_doc: GOLD-FAIL before/after fixtures: every `after` here is a BAD rewrite that a correct LLM judge must reject (expected_verdict='FAIL'). WHY A SEPARATE FILE (not appended to fixtures.json): the 13 fixtures in fixtures.json are all good rewrites (expected_verdict='PASS'); their `before` items feed the naive-baseline miss-rate calibration (the 30-40% band). These FAIL fixtures test the ORTHOGONAL question — judge DISCRIMINATION: when handed a bad rewrite, does the judge say FAIL? They are NOT calibration items and MUST NOT enter the naive-baseline denominator, so they live in their own file and are loaded by their own runner path (run_fixtures.run_fail_deterministic / run_fail_judge). Keeping them out of fixtures.json is what preserves the 3/8 = 38% band untouched — do NOT move them in to 'unify' the suites. Fields: `expected_verdict` is always 'FAIL' (the gold label). `fail_dimension` names the rubric dimension a correct judge drives the FAIL through — it must appear in `rubric_focus` (so the judge is actually told to weigh it) unless it is `no_fabrication`, which the rubric requires on every verdict even when unlisted. `why` explains the defect in one line. Optional `before_band_max`/`after_band_max` are LOOSE detector regression guards (these strings are not detector-targeted — most score ~0 — the band just stops them silently rotting into vocabulary tells). HOW THE TWO PATHS USE THESE: the DETERMINISTIC path (CI, key-free) only validates well-formedness + score bands here — it cannot compute a FAIL verdict without a judge. The JUDGE path (AIWS_JUDGE_RUN=1, opt-in) scores each `after`, aggregates the verdict, and compares to the gold: judge-says-FAIL == agreement (correct discrimination); judge-says-PASS == DISAGREEMENT (the judge failed to catch a bad rewrite — the signal this suite exists to surface). Coverage includes at minimum the three archetypes from the hardening plan: fabricated fact (after invents a stat absent from before), over-corrected over-stepping (removal guts a legitimate contrast whose prior was a real belief), and a payoff_clear stub (over-stripping strands a pronoun with no antecedent).

fixtures.before: Our new caching layer makes the dashboard load faster and improves the experience for everyone.
fixtures.after: Our new caching layer cut dashboard load time by 37%, from 2.1s to 1.3s.
fixtures.why: `before` contains no numbers; `after` invents 37% and the 2.1s -> 1.3s figures and presents them as measured fact. Fluent and specific, but fabricated -> no_fabrication FAIL (the highest-stakes dimension) forces the overall verdict FAIL regardless of how good the prose reads. A judge that rewards the added specificity would wrongly PASS this.

fixtures.before: Love isn't just a feeling, it's a daily choice you keep making.
fixtures.after: Love is a daily choice you keep making.
fixtures.why: The over-correction trap. 'Love = a feeling' is a REAL, widespread prior, so 'isn't just a feeling, it's a choice' is a LEGITIMATE contrast carrying information — not a manufactured strawman. `after` stripped the contrast, losing that information. A naive judge that rewards removing ANY 'X isn't Y' frame would wrongly PASS; the validity condition (over-stepping is real ONLY when the prior is manufactured) must drive meaning_preserved FAIL.

fixtures.before: You might think merging the two staging environments causes more outages, but it actually reduces them: one config to keep in sync instead of two, and we traced 3 of the last 5 incidents to drift between them.
fixtures.after: It actually reduces them: one config to keep in sync instead of two, and we traced 3 of the last 5 incidents to drift between them.
fixtures.why: The live payoff_clear stub — the minimal-pair FAIL partner of fixtures.json::overstep-05. Deleting the presumption was right, but the over-stripped leftover 'It actually reduces them' strands 'it'/'them': the antecedent (merging the two staging environments) lived only in the deleted frame. overstepping_removed reads PASS, but payoff_clear FAIL forces the overall verdict FAIL. The clean fix names the subject ('Merging reduces outages: ...').

fixtures.before: We're behind on Q3 by 14% for two reasons: the API migration slipped three weeks and we lost two engineers.
fixtures.after: We're behind on Q3 for a couple of reasons and we're working on it.
fixtures.why: Over-correction by deletion: `after` drops the 14% figure and both concrete causes (API migration slip, two engineers lost), replacing them with 'a couple of reasons' and empty reassurance. This is the opposite failure from fabrication — the rewrite is honest but gutted the load-bearing claims -> meaning_preserved FAIL.

fixtures.before: The nightly backup job is green again. The fix was a one-character typo in the cron schedule.
fixtures.after: The nightly backup job is green again, for now. The fix was a one-character typo in the cron schedule, though I keep wondering whether the real story is messier, whether something deeper in how we think about scheduling is unresolved, and whether fixed is ever really the right word.
fixtures.why: OVER-CORRECTION of the narrative-shape category. The `before` is a genuinely simple, single-outcome update: the thread is truly closed (a one-character cron typo, now green). Its tidy resolution is MOTIVATED (validity condition) -> NOT a tell. The `after` manufactures fake residue and fake ambiguity ('for now', 'the real story is messier', 'whether fixed is ever really the right word') purely to LOOK human -- injected complexity where the material supplies none. A naive judge might reward 'it left a thread open, good'; the validity condition forbids that. narrative_shape_ok: FAIL (over-correction). This is the failure mode the category must ship paired with, exactly as over-stepping ships its 不是X而是Y over-strip hard-negatives.
